title: What are some of the key features introduced in Java 8?

answers.junior: Java 8 introduced several transformative features that fundamentally changed how Java developers write code, with lambda expressions being the most significant. Lambda expressions enable functional programming in Java by allowing you to pass behavior as data, making code more concise and expressive; for example, list.stream().filter(x -> x > 5).collect(Collectors.toList()) is far cleaner than pre-Java 8 anonymous inner class syntax.
answers.middle: The Stream API, introduced alongside lambdas, provides a declarative way to process collections with functional operations like map, filter, reduce, and collect, enabling parallel processing through streams without explicitly managing threads. Functional interfaces, marked with @FunctionalInterface, define contracts for lambda expressions and method references, with common examples including Function<T, R>, Predicate<T>, and Consumer<T>. Method references provide an even more concise syntax when you want to reference existing methods; for instance, array.stream().forEach(System.out::println) is cleaner than using a lambda.
answers.senior: Default methods in interfaces allow adding new methods to interfaces without breaking existing implementations, enabling evolution of APIs while maintaining backward compatibility. The Optional<T> class addresses the billion-dollar null pointer exception problem by providing a container that explicitly represents the presence or absence of a value, encouraging safer null handling with methods like ifPresentOrElse() and orElseGet(). The java.time package replaced the notoriously problematic java.util.Date with immutable, thread-safe classes like LocalDateTime, ZonedDateTime, and Instant, following industry-standard JSR-310.
answers.tricky: CompletableFuture brought first-class support for asynchronous programming, allowing you to compose asynchronous operations with thenApply(), thenCompose(), and other combinators, which is essential for non-blocking I/O in modern applications. These features collectively shifted Java from a primarily imperative, object-oriented language toward a more functional programming paradigm, improving code readability, maintainability, and enabling better performance optimizations through lazy evaluation and parallelization.
